title: Page Writer
description: An HTTP service where Claude writes HTML pages that deploy as durable resources. You POST a prompt, Claude writes a complete standalone document, and the app deploys it to disk as a managed resource. By the end you can restart the process at will and watch every page come back ready without a single regeneration.
setup_intro: The app reads two environment variables: <0>ANTHROPIC_API_KEY</0> authenticates the Anthropic SDK and <1>PORT</1> selects the server port, defaulting to 3000. Copy the template and export your key:
run_intro: <0>npm start</0> runs <1>creact --watch index.tsx</1>. Watch mode keeps the process alive to serve requests and restarts it when a source file changes — plain <2>creact index.tsx</2> converges once and exits, which is no way to run a server:
run_create_intro: Once the log shows the server listening, create a page:
code_curl_create:
curl -X POST http://localhost:3000/pages \
  -H 'content-type: application/json' \
  -d '{"prompt": "a landing page for a sourdough bakery"}'
run_create_outro: The response arrives immediately while Claude writes in the background:
run_list_intro: List every page and its deployment state:
run_list_outro: Each entry reports <0>state</0> as <1>writing</1>, <2>ready</2>, or <3>failed</3>, the deployed <4>file</4> once ready, and <5>latest: true</5> on the newest page that finished deploying. Open the file under <6>./out</6> straight in a browser.
tree_intro: The whole service is one small tree — an HTTP channel that accepts prompts, and a fleet of <0>Page</0> resources grown from a durable request ledger:
tree_outro: Notice what is missing: no route handler ever writes a file. The server only records requests; <0>For</0> turns each recorded request into a <1>Page</1> keyed by its slug, and the runtime deploys whatever is new. Declaring a page is what creates it.
fail_fast_intro: The entry point refuses to start without the one credential the app cannot work without:
fail_fast_outro: A missing key stops the process at boot with instructions, not as a failed generation after the first request. The <0>FileMemory</0> ledger under <1>./.state</1> is the persistence backend every durable resource in this walkthrough writes through.
ledger_intro: Requests must survive restarts, so they live in a resource's outputs instead of a plain signal:
ledger_outro: The handler restores <0>requests</0> from the previously persisted value on every startup, and <1>append</1> reuses the same <2>setOutputs</2> to persist each new request the moment it arrives. Because the ledger drives <3>For</3>, a restarted process re-mounts a <4>Page</4> for every remembered request before any HTTP traffic arrives.
wiring_intro: One hook joins the ledger, the live page states, and the two HTTP callbacks:
wiring_outro: <0>add</0> dedupes by slug, so posting the same prompt twice returns the same page instead of paying for two generations. <1>createSelector</1> answers whether a slug is the latest ready page — when the answer changes, only the two entries whose answer flipped recompute, not the whole list.
http_server_intro: The server itself is a resource — its <0>useAsyncOutput</0> handler resolves once the socket is listening:
http_server_outro: The handler returns a cleanup that closes the server, so unmounting the channel or restarting in watch mode releases the port before the next process claims it.
http_routes_intro: Routing is two plain functions, no framework:
http_routes_outro: The POST route validates its body — malformed JSON and missing prompts return <0>400</0> — and answers <1>202</1> straight away. The client already holds its slug while the page deploys in the background.
http_teardown_intro: Two helpers keep responses and shutdown symmetric:
http_teardown_outro: <0>closeServer</0> resolves only after the listener has fully closed, which keeps a watch-mode restart from racing the old process for the port.
lifecycle_states_intro: A page moves through <0>writing</0>, <1>ready</1>, and <2>failed</2>, and <3>Switch</3> renders exactly one status line at a time:
lifecycle_states_outro: <0>createRenderEffect</0> reports every state change to the parent synchronously, before effects run. <1>getOwner</1> captures the component's scope up front so the async handler can re-enter it after its awaits.
lifecycle_handler_intro: The handler is where durability earns its keep:
lifecycle_handler_outro: The restore is the highlight: <0>setOutputs</0> hands the handler its previously persisted outputs, and when the page was already <1>ready</1> it returns before ever reaching Claude. A restart re-runs every handler, and every settled page takes this early exit — zero API calls. Only genuinely new requests pay for <2>writeHtml</2>.
lifecycle_write_intro: Deploying is an ordinary filesystem write plus a scope re-entry:
lifecycle_write_outro: <0>runWithOwner</0> re-enters the scope captured before the awaits, so <1>onCleanup</1> registers against the page component instead of nowhere. When a page later unmounts, the note reminds you that the HTML file stays on disk — detaching a resource is not destroying its artifact.
claude_client_intro: The Anthropic wrapper narrows the SDK to the single call the app makes:
claude_client_outro: The <0>HtmlWriterClient</0> interface is the seam for tests — a scripted client exercises the whole pipeline without a network or an API key.
claude_request_intro: One request turns a prompt into a full document:
claude_request_outro: The system prompt pins the contract: one standalone document starting at <0><!DOCTYPE html></0>, styles inlined, no markdown fences. The reply is written to disk verbatim, so everything the page needs must be in it.
claude_extract_intro: Two pure helpers finish the pipeline:
claude_extract_outro: <0>extractHtmlDocument</0> throws when Claude returns no text block, and that throw lands in the page's <1>failed</1> state instead of a half-written file. <2>slugify</2> is deterministic — the same prompt always maps to the same slug, which is exactly what makes duplicate requests collapse into one page.
settle_intro: Every accepted request gets a self-disposing observer that logs when its page settles:
settle_outro: <0>createRoot</0> creates a detached scope with its own <1>dispose</1>. The effect watches the page's state until it reaches <2>ready</2> or <3>failed</3>, logs once, then disposes its whole scope — no leaked subscription, no polling loop.
checkpoint_dedupe: POST the same bakery prompt a second time. The response carries the same slug and no second generation starts — the ledger already holds that request, so <0>add</0> returns the existing page.
checkpoint_restart: Now stop the process and start it again. The ledger restores its request list, <0>For</0> re-mounts every <1>Page</1>, and each handler restores <2>ready</2> from persisted outputs — the whole fleet is back with zero Claude calls. Delete <3>./.state</3> only when you genuinely want to regenerate everything.
recap_ledger: Requests live in a resource's outputs, so the request list is itself durable state.
recap_fleet: <0>For</0> grows one <1>Page</1> per ledger entry — declaring a page is what deploys it.
recap_server: The HTTP server is a resource whose cleanup releases the port on unmount or restart.
recap_restore: Handlers restore before they generate, so restarts cost zero API calls.
recap_owner: <0>getOwner</0> and <1>runWithOwner</1> carry the component scope across awaits so <2>onCleanup</2> lands in the right place.
recap_root: <0>createRoot</0> gives one-off watchers a scope they can dispose themselves.
challenge_body: Change the <0>SYSTEM_PROMPT</0> in <1>src/claude/html-writer/index.ts</1> — demand dark-mode pages or a strict typographic style. Restart, POST a fresh prompt, and compare the new page against the ones written under the old prompt. The old pages stay exactly as they were: only new requests flow through the new prompt, because settled pages never regenerate.
